judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports discover the prior reverted attempt and the connection-exhaustion cause, but Report 2 answers better with precise commit citations (454a21f, 597777b, 1712565), a verbatim commit body, and explicit recognition that the migration was already tried and reverted.

report_1.evidence: The team already encountered this failure in April 2026 (commit `1712565`): when they tried serverless, Postgres connection limit was exhausted under normal load, taking the endpoint down

report_2.evidence: This was already attempted in production (commits 454a21f through 1712565) and failed with a service outage.

report_1.evidence: The team already encountered this failure in April 2026 (commit `1712565`): when they tried serverless, Postgres connection limit was exhausted under normal load, taking the endpoint down. Simply removing pooling (per request) made it worse, not better—connections opened faster than Postgres could recycle them

report_2.evidence: Commit 1712565 states "Under normal dashboard load the per-request connections opened faster than Postgres could recycle them and tripped the connection limit ('FATAL: too many connections')"
Connection pool exhaustion (SETTLED BATTLE - already tried and reverted) ... This was already attempted in production (commits 454a21f through 1712565) and failed with a service outage.

report_1.evidence: Proven failure in this codebase: The team already attempted this migration and it failed in production (April 2026). ... the decision should involve: Engineering Lead / Architecture ... Database Administrator / Ops ... Incident Responders / On-Call: Document that a prior attempt failed with connection exhaustion

report_2.evidence: Settled battle: This was already attempted in production ... Decision maker: This decision should involve: Platform/Infrastructure team: They own the Cargobox vs. Edgerunner trade-off ... Engineering leadership ... Database team / DBA
